title: runtime/cgo: pthread_create failed: Resource temporarily unavailable
body:
Am trying to use skopeo on a Raspberry Pi Model B (256MB ram) and skopeo seems a tad memory hungry.. I've cross-compiled it with go version go1.13.8 linux/arm (this is an ARMv6 processor which is 32-bit). 

In general it starts fine but i find it consumes almost all host memory and eventually dies.  I tried limiting its memory usage with cgroups but then it gets killed.

Thanks for opening the issue, @aleks-mariusz! I fear that 256MB are really tough to support.

Can you try `skopeo copy --dest-oci-accept-uncompressed-layers containers-storage:... oci:...` and report if that worked? At least one dump indicates that we ran out of memory while recompressing the layers.

Well, i gave the `--dest-oci-accept-uncompressed-layers` flag a try and it worked, however really this highly is transport-specific and for example, when trying to upload layers to dockerhub it fails just as badly.  I was looking high and low for a way to, as a start, _not_ do parallel uploads there, and gave up finally so _that's_ what lead me to finally try to use an intermediate system via OCI (which is what caused this original issue).

Anyway, it may be interesting to note that formerly before i knew about this flag, while running `htop` during a `skopeo copy` session, i saw it using more and more memory the further layers it got to.  I can look into recording a split screen live terminal video of htop/skopeo running that shows this behaviour if you think it'll be useful.. 

What's also interesting prior to using that flag is, out of stubbornness, I ran skopeo copy repeatedly, and after several dozen attempts, eventually it _did finish_ (even tho it didn't seem like it ever resumed any existing layers since it probably cleans the destination up on failure)... 

That's a bit disheartening seeing that skopeo wasn't really designed with embedded systems/resource-limited environments in mind.  It's easy to take memory for granted in today's multi-gigabyte systems but that also can too easily lead to inefficient design..  FWIW, i've been using my Pi on and off since it came out in 2012, and skopeo is the _first_ workload I've thrown at my Pi where it fails this badly.. of course i won't bother doing things such as compiling anything other than a hello world on the Pi, but simply copying over layers not supported with only 256MB of ram is really dissappointing.. Am i just under-estimating the complexity of these operations?

So my follow-ups are, yes that flag works as a workaround, but is oci-specific so what i'd really like to see is if we can a.) explore memory usage patterns, if there's perhaps a memory leak (based on the observed behavior that memory usage increases continually with the number of layers processed), and finally b.) look into adding more flags for resource-constrained environments (and c.) ideally detect them and turn those optimizations on).

Both the second and third traces point at `layerStore.Diff` reading decompressing all of the tar-split metadata into memory (which is 42 MB on the largest layer I happen to have laying around), and then decoding that into in-memory JSON (which is probably several times that). So, it’s not altogether surprising that this runs out of 256 MB.

Frankly, that’s bad, and it’s not great that no-one has noticed before.

(But the, to be clear, it’s not 100% certain that _this_ is the problem you are running into. Actual measurements instead of guessing based on the backtrace might indicate otherwise.)

OTOH, given that no-one has noticed before, it’s somewhat unlikely to be fixed on its own. If you are interested in working on this, we’d be happy to accept clean PRs. Ideal would be just decreasing the memory usage without having to add options, but I can imagine some options or or maybe autodetection (it is already possible to decrease the level of compression, and if we really could significantly decrease memory usage by tuning down the parallelism, that might be worth considering).

> and then decoding that into in-memory JSON (which is probably several times that).

Actually, not this; `JSONUnpacker` is a streaming helper.
